summary:
Pantothenic acid or Vitamin B5 is essential as it forms part of CoA, crucial for metabolic pathways including glycolysis where glycogen phosphorylase acts by breaking down stored glycogen into glucose molecules needed for cellular respiration. The direct link between this vitamin and enzyme function supports its necessity here.
 To improve my response I will focus more succinctly on the core point about vitamins involved directly with enzymatic functions related specifically to glycogen metabolism without extraneous details such as broader roles in other metabolic pathways not pertinent to answering 'which specific vitamin'.
 
Revised Summary:

Pantothenic Acid/Vitamin B5 is necessary because it composes CoA, vital for activating acyl groups used in various steps within carbohydrate catabolism; particularly important since glycogen phosphorylase needs these activated intermediates from CoA for effective debranching reactions leading to glucose production. This highlights why only pantothenic acid fits precisely what was asked regarding an explicit connection to glycogen processing through phosphorylase activity. 

The summary has been condensed while maintaining all critical elements focused solely around explaining how/why pantothenic acid meets requirements specified in relation to glycogen phosphorylase action mechanisms most clearly possible under two sentence constraints provided initially. No additional context beyond immediate relevance remains included now either. Agent
To summarize effectively in just two sentences focusing strictly on the key clinical points:

Vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid) is required for glycogen phosphorylase because it forms coenzymes integral to substrate activation in carbohydrate metabolism, enabling proper functioning of phosphorolytic activities in glycogen degradation. Thus, ensuring adequate levels maintains optimal performance of glycogen phosphorylase in gluconeogenesis.
